content:
# Luke Chapter 8 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 95-97 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_008_page_001.jpg, LUK_008_page_002.jpg, LUK_008_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass afterward that he was going through every city and village, proclaiming and bringing the good news of the kingdom of God, and the twelve were with him,

**2** and certain women who had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities: Mary who is called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had gone out,

**3** and Joanna the wife of Chuza, Herod's steward, and Susanna, and many others who were ministering to them out of their own resources.

**4** And when a great crowd was gathering together, with people from every city coming to him, he spoke through a parable:

**5** "The sower went out to sow his seed. And as he sowed, some fell along the path and was trampled underfoot, and the birds of heaven devoured it.

**6** And some fell upon the rock, and having sprung up, it withered because it had no moisture.

**7** And some fell in the midst of the thorns, and the thorns, having grown up together with it, choked it.

**8** And some fell into the good earth, and having sprung up, it produced fruit a hundredfold." Saying these things, he called out, "He who has ears to hear, let him hear."

**9** And his disciples asked him what this parable might mean.

**10** And he said, "To you it has been given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of God, but to the rest it is given in parables, so that seeing they may not see, and hearing they may not understand.

**11** Now the parable is this: The seed is the word of God.

**12** And those along the path are those who have heard; then the devil comes and takes away the word from their heart, so that they may not believe and be saved.

**13** And those upon the rock are those who, when they hear, receive the word with joy; and these have no root, who believe for a time and in a time of testing fall away.

**14** And that which fell among the thorns, these are those who have heard, but as they go on their way they are choked by the cares and riches and pleasures of life, and they do not bring fruit to maturity.

**15** And that in the good earth, these are those who, having heard the word with a noble and good heart, hold it fast and bear fruit with patient endurance.

**16** No one, having lit a lamp, covers it with a vessel or places it under a bed, but sets it upon a lampstand, so that those who enter may see the light.

**17** For there is nothing hidden that shall not become manifest, nor anything secret that shall not be known and come into the open.

**18** Take heed therefore how you hear; for whoever has, to him more shall be given, and whoever does not have, even what he thinks he has shall be taken from him."

**19** And his mother and his brothers came to him, but they were not able to reach him because of the crowd.

**20** And it was reported to him, "Your mother and your brothers are standing outside, wanting to see you."

**21** But he answered and said to them, "My mother and my brothers are these who hear the word of God and do it."

**22** And it came to pass on one of the days that he got into a boat, he and his disciples, and he said to them, "Let us cross over to the other side of the lake." And they set out.

**23** And as they were sailing, he fell asleep. And a violent windstorm came down upon the lake, and they were being swamped and were in danger.

**24** And they came to him and woke him, saying, "Master, Master, we are perishing!" And he, having risen, rebuked the wind and the surging of the water; and they ceased, and a calm came.

**25** And he said to them, "Where is your faith?" And being afraid, they marveled, saying to one another, "Who then is this, that he commands even the winds and the water, and they obey him?"

**26** And they sailed to the region of the Gerasenes, which is opposite Galilee.

**27** And when he went out upon the land, a certain man from the city met him who had demons, and for a considerable time he had not put on a garment, and he was not dwelling in a house but among the tombs.

**28** And seeing Jesus, he cried out and fell down before him, and said with a loud voice, "What have I to do with you, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I beg you, do not torment me."

**29** For he had commanded the unclean spirit to come out of the man. For many times it had seized him, and he was kept under guard, bound with chains and shackles, and breaking the bonds, he was driven by the demon into the desolate places.

**30** And Jesus asked him, "What is your name?" And he said, "Legion," for many demons had entered into him.

**31** And they were entreating him that he would not command them to go away into the abyss.

**32** Now there was there a herd of many pigs feeding on the mountain. And they entreated him that he would permit them to enter into those. And he permitted them.

**33** And the demons, having gone out of the man, entered into the pigs, and the herd rushed down the steep bank into the lake and were drowned.

**34** And when those who were feeding them saw what had happened, they fled and reported it in the city and in the countryside.

**35** And they went out to see what had happened, and they came to Jesus and found the man from whom the demons had gone out sitting at the feet of Jesus, clothed and in his right mind. And they were afraid.

**36** And those who had seen it reported to them how the demon-possessed man had been saved.

**37** And the whole multitude of the surrounding region of the Gerasenes asked him to depart from them, for they were seized with great fear. And he got into a boat and returned.

**38** And the man from whom the demons had gone out was begging him that he might be with him. But he sent him away, saying,

**39** "Return to your house and declare what great things God has done for you." And he went away, proclaiming throughout the whole city what great things Jesus had done for him.

**40** And when Jesus returned, the crowd welcomed him, for they were all expecting him.

**41** And behold, there came a man whose name was Jairus, and this man was a ruler of the synagogue. And he fell at the feet of Jesus and was entreating him to come into his house,

**42** for he had an only daughter, about twelve years of age, and she was dying. And as he was going, the crowds were pressing upon him.

**43** And a woman, having had a flow of blood for twelve years, who had spent all her living on physicians and could not be healed by anyone,

**44** came up behind him and touched the fringe of his garment, and immediately the flow of her blood stopped.

**45** And Jesus said, "Who is the one who touched me?" And when all were denying it, Peter said, "Master, the crowds are pressing against you and crushing you."

**46** But Jesus said, "Someone touched me, for I perceived that power had gone out from me."

**47** And the woman, seeing that she had not escaped notice, came trembling, and falling down before him, she declared before all the people the reason she had touched him, and how she was healed immediately.

**48** And he said to her, "Daughter, your faith has saved you. Go in peace."

**49** While he was still speaking, someone comes from the house of the ruler of the synagogue, saying, "Your daughter has died; do not trouble the teacher any longer."

**50** But Jesus, hearing this, answered him, "Do not fear; only believe, and she shall be saved."

**51** And when he came to the house, he did not allow anyone to enter with him except Peter and John and James, and the father of the child and the mother.

**52** And all were weeping and mourning for her. But he said, "Do not weep, for she has not died but is sleeping."

**53** And they were laughing at him, knowing that she had died.

**54** But he, having taken hold of her hand, called out, saying, "Child, arise."

**55** And her spirit returned, and she rose up at once. And he directed that something be given to her to eat.

**56** And her parents were amazed. But he charged them to tell no one what had happened.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The three Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 95-97) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 8:1-21 across four columns. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 8:27-44. Page 3 covers approximately Luke 8:52 through the beginning of Luke 9. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΑΥΤΟΙΣ (8:3, "to them" — plural, not "to him")
- ΔΙΑ ΠΑΡΑΒΟΛΗΣ (8:4, singular "a parable")
- ΕΚΑΤΟΝΤΑΠΛΑΣΙΟΝΑ (8:8, "hundredfold" — singular yield, no graduated sequence)
- ΓΕΡΑΣΗΝΩΝ (8:26 and 8:37, "Gerasenes" consistently)
- ΕΝ ΤΩ ΟΡΕΙ (8:32, "on the mountain")
- ΠΕΤΡΟΣ (8:45, Peter alone, not "Peter and those with him")
- ΕΓΝΩΝ (8:46, "I perceived/knew" — aorist, without the emphatic "I myself")
- ΕΡΧΕΤΑΙ (8:49, historical present "comes")
- ΠΕΤΡΟΝ ΚΑΙ ΙΩΑΝΝΗΝ ΚΑΙ ΙΑΚΩΒΟΝ (8:51, "Peter and John and James" — Lukan order)

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **8:3 — "to them" (autois):** Sinaiticus reads the plural ΑΥΤΟΙΣ, indicating the women ministered to Jesus and the twelve collectively. All three agents note this variant; SOPHIA preserves the Sinaiticus reading.

2. **8:8 — "He who has ears to hear":** EMET renders "The one having ears to hear," LOGOS "He who has ears to hear," PNEUMA "Whoever has ears to hear, let them hear!" SOPHIA follows LOGOS's formal rendering, which preserves the masculine singular of the Greek while maintaining solemnity. PNEUMA's plural "them" is more inclusive but departs from the Greek singular.

3. **8:14 — "bring fruit to maturity":** EMET renders "bring fruit to completion," LOGOS "bring fruit to maturity," PNEUMA "produce mature fruit." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "maturity" better captures the agricultural metaphor of *telesphorousin* (bearing fruit to its full term) than "completion."

4. **8:15 — "noble and good heart":** All three agents agree on the pairing *kale kai agathe* as "noble and good" (or "honest and good" for PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS with "noble and good," preserving the echo of the Greek philosophical ideal *kalos kagathos* noted by PNEUMA.

5. **8:18 — "Take heed" vs. "See then" vs. "Pay attention":** EMET renders "See then," LOGOS "Take heed therefore," PNEUMA "So pay attention." SOPHIA adopts "Take heed therefore" as the rendering that best balances formality with clarity, preserving the imperative force of *blepete*.

6. **8:24 — "surging" of the water:** EMET renders "raging," LOGOS "surge," PNEUMA "raging waves." The Greek *kludoni* refers to the surging or billowing of water. SOPHIA renders "surging of the water" — close to LOGOS, precise to the Greek noun, and avoiding PNEUMA's interpretive addition of "waves."

7. **8:28 — "What have I to do with you":** EMET renders "What is there to me and to you," LOGOS "What have I to do with you," PNEUMA "What do you want with me." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — this preserves the Semitic idiom (*ti emoi kai soi*) in its traditional English form, maintaining both the foreignness and the force of the expression.

8. **8:32 — "pigs" vs. "swine":** EMET renders "pigs," LOGOS "swine," PNEUMA "pigs." SOPHIA adopts "pigs" (2-to-1) as the more natural modern English term. The Greek *choiron* is the common word for pigs; "swine" is archaic.

9. **8:36 — "saved" (esothe):** EMET renders "was saved," LOGOS "had been saved," PNEUMA "had been made well." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS in rendering *esothe* as "saved" rather than "made well." The Greek *sozo* carries both physical healing and spiritual salvation; "saved" preserves this dual meaning, consistent with its use in 8:48 and 8:50.

10. **8:42 — "only daughter" vs. "only-begotten daughter":** EMET renders "only-born," LOGOS "only-begotten," PNEUMA "only." SOPHIA adopts "only daughter" — *monogenes* means "one of a kind, only" (the "begotten" nuance is disputed in modern scholarship). "Only daughter" is clear and accurate, and the theological weight of *monogenes* in Johannine usage does not apply with the same force in this narrative context.

11. **8:46 — "I perceived":** EMET renders "I knew" (*egnon*), LOGOS "I perceived," PNEUMA "I felt." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "perceived" captures the aorist *egnon* (a moment of knowing/sensing) better than "knew" (which implies ongoing knowledge) or "felt" (which is too subjective for the Greek verb *ginosko*).

12. **8:48 — "saved" (sesoken):** EMET and LOGOS render "has saved you"; PNEUMA renders "has made you well." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS — the Greek *sesoken* (perfect of *sozo*) carries the full range of "save/heal/make whole." Rendering it as "saved" maintains the theological resonance present in the Greek and provides consistency with the same verb in 7:50, 8:36, and 8:50.

13. **8:49 — Historical present "comes":** EMET and LOGOS preserve the historical present tense *erchetai* ("comes"). PNEUMA normalizes to past tense ("came"). SOPHIA preserves the historical present, which creates the vivid, immediate narrative effect that Luke employs at this dramatic moment.

14. **8:51 — Disciple order "Peter and John and James":** All three agents correctly follow Sinaiticus's distinctive Lukan ordering, placing John before James. This differs from the typical Synoptic order and is characteristic of Luke-Acts.

15. **8:52 — "has not died but is sleeping":** EMET renders "did not die, but she sleeps," LOGOS "has not died but is sleeping," PNEUMA "is not dead — she is sleeping." SOPHIA follows LOGOS for the most natural English construction that preserves both tenses accurately.

16. **8:54 — "arise" vs. "get up":** EMET and LOGOS render "arise"; PNEUMA renders "get up!" SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS — "arise" preserves the solemnity and the connection to resurrection language (*egeire*), consistent with the SOPHIA decision at Luke 7:14.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 8:4 | Crowd description | "those from each city journeying to him" | "those from every city were coming to him" | "people streaming to him from town after town" | "people from every city coming to him" | Blends LOGOS accuracy with PNEUMA naturalness |
| 8:5 | Sower rendering | "The one sowing" | "The sower" | "A farmer" | "The sower" | Greek *ho speiron* is a participle used as noun; "sower" is the standard equivalent; "farmer" is too broad |
| 8:8 | Ears formula | "The one having ears to hear, let him hear" | "He who has ears to hear, let him hear" | "Whoever has ears to hear, let them hear!" | "He who has ears to hear, let him hear" | Preserves Greek masculine singular; formal register |
| 8:14 | Fruit completion | "bring fruit to completion" | "bring fruit to maturity" | "produce mature fruit" | "bring fruit to maturity" | "Maturity" captures the agricultural metaphor of *telesphoreo* |
| 8:15 | Heart adjectives | "noble and good" | "noble and good" | "honest and good" | "noble and good" | 2-to-1; *kalos* = noble/beautiful, not merely "honest" |
| 8:18 | Opening imperative | "See then" | "Take heed therefore" | "So pay attention" | "Take heed therefore" | Formal register; preserves force of *blepete* |
| 8:20 | "wanting" vs "wishing" | "wanting to see you" | "wishing to see you" | "wanting to see you" | "wanting to see you" | 2-to-1; "wanting" is more natural |
| 8:23 | Storm description | "a storm of wind" | "a violent windstorm" | "a fierce windstorm" | "a violent windstorm" | LOGOS captures *lailaps anemou* with appropriate force |
| 8:24 | Water description | "the raging of the water" | "the surge of the water" | "the raging waves" | "the surging of the water" | *kludon* = surging/billowing; SOPHIA blends noun form with LOGOS's lexical choice |
| 8:25 | Fear description | "having been afraid, they marveled" | "being afraid, they marveled" | "Shaken and amazed" | "being afraid, they marveled" | Preserves the two-stage Greek response; PNEUMA compresses too much |
| 8:28 | Semitic idiom | "What is there to me and to you" | "What have I to do with you" | "What do you want with me" | "What have I to do with you" | Traditional rendering preserves Semitic idiom |
| 8:29 | Bonds/shackles | "chains and fetters" | "chains and shackles" | "chains and shackles" | "chains and shackles" | 2-to-1; "shackles" is more immediately clear than "fetters" |
| 8:32 | Animal term | "pigs" | "swine" | "pigs" | "pigs" | 2-to-1; "swine" is archaic; Greek *choiroi* is the common term |
| 8:32 | Location | "on the mountain" | "on the mountain" | "on the hillside" | "on the mountain" | 2-to-1; Sinaiticus reads *en to orei* ("on the mountain") |
| 8:33 | Herd motion | "rushed down the steep bank" | "rushed down the steep bank" | "stampeded down the steep bank" | "rushed down the steep bank" | 2-to-1; *hormesen* = rushed/charged; "stampeded" is interpretive |
| 8:36 | *esothe* rendering | "was saved" | "had been saved" | "had been made well" | "had been saved" | *sozo* = save/heal; "saved" preserves dual meaning; PNEUMA's "made well" reduces it |
| 8:37 | Departure verb | "go away" | "depart" | "leave" | "depart" | "Depart" is formal and precise; fits the solemnity of the communal rejection |
| 8:39 | "what things" vs "what great things" | "what things" | "what great things" | "everything" | "what great things" | LOGOS adds *hosa* ("how great things"); follows variant tradition also present in LOGOS reading |
| 8:42 | *monogenes* | "only-born" | "only-begotten" | "only" | "only daughter" | Modern scholarship favors "only/unique" for *monogenes*; "only daughter" is clear and accurate |
| 8:46 | *egnon* rendering | "I knew" | "I perceived" | "I felt" | "I perceived" | "Perceived" captures the moment of awareness; "felt" is too subjective; "knew" implies ongoing state |
| 8:48 | *sesoken* rendering | "has saved you" | "has saved you" | "has made you well" | "has saved you" | 2-to-1; preserves the full *sozo* range; consistent with 7:50 |
| 8:49 | Verb tense | "comes" (hist. present) | "comes" (hist. present) | "came" (past) | "comes" (hist. present) | 2-to-1; preserves Sinaiticus's vivid *erchetai* |
| 8:50 | *sothesetai* rendering | "she shall be saved" | "she shall be saved" | "she will be made well" | "she shall be saved" | 2-to-1; consistency with *sozo* throughout the chapter |
| 8:52 | Death/sleep statement | "she did not die, but she sleeps" | "she has not died but is sleeping" | "She is not dead -- she is sleeping" | "she has not died but is sleeping" | LOGOS rendering best preserves Greek tenses |
| 8:54 | Command to child | "Child, arise" | "Child, arise" | "Child, get up!" | "Child, arise" | 2-to-1; "arise" preserves resurrection solemnity; consistent with 7:14 |
| 8:55 | Rising description | "rose up immediately" | "rose up at once" | "stood up at once" | "rose up at once" | LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "at once"; *aneste* = "rose up" not merely "stood up" |
| 8:56 | Parental response | "were astonished" | "were amazed" | "were astonished" | "were amazed" | LOGOS's "amazed" for *exestesan* is precise; the verb denotes being beside oneself with wonder |

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
